title: Lesson 423
transcript: Okay, let's continue our discussion of some of the controls that we can use in physical security. And we're going to continue on here next by talking about CCTV, closed circuit television, camera systems. Now, we used to go into a bunch of detail on different transmission media and camera quality, things like that, but I don't think you'll be tested on that anymore. What you might be tested on, and a good thing that you should know about, is what type of control is a camera system? Is it a detective control? Is it a deterrent control? Is it a sort of an audit control? What type of control is a camera system? The answer is all of those, right? Camera provides all those capabilities. Camera systems are absolutely good deterrents. Cameras are obviously good detective control. They even, to a sense, can kind of provide like an audit capability. You can see who came and went when. So cameras are all of those things. So here's a couple of things I would keep in mind for the exam. Number one. You might be given a question that asks you, which of the following types of controls is a camera system primarily? Deterrent, detective, corrective, et cetera. If you're forced to choose one thing above all else, the answer is detective. Above all else, camera systems are primarily a detective control. So remember that, above all else, camera systems are primarily detective controls. But like many controls, they can serve multiple purposes. So camera systems are absolutely a good deterrent. In fact, you've all seen this, right? You walk into a sketchy gas station corner store or something, right? And there's often like a big camera, right? When you walk in with a screen that says, you know, you are being recorded. They want you to know that you're on camera. They want you to know that you're being recorded because they're trying to deter you from stealing stuff. So cameras are absolutely a good deterrent, and they're often used for that exact purpose. They're put up in super visual places to deter people from committing crimes, doing things that they shouldn't do. They also are quite useful from an audit perspective. Like if you want to sort of see who came and went when. Maybe someone went into your store with an empty backpack and then they came out with a big bulging backpack. They probably stole your stuff. So they can be used sort of for audit purposes as well. So the reason I'm pointing this out is that you might see another type of question which asks you, you know, which are the following types of controls as a camera system. And you're given a number of choices. In that case, remember that cameras are absolutely a good deterrent. They're also a good detective control. They're also a good audit type control. So that's camera systems. Let's now talk about these passive infrared devices. And I gotta be careful how I say infrared. It's actually infrared with three syllables. And I know that because I recorded a mind map video and I put it up on YouTube and some kind soul on YouTube took a large portion of their day to write me like a huge explanation on why infrared is actually three syllables they included the latin derivation everything else so i don't want to piss that person off on youtube so Infrared, passive infrared devices. So what are passive infrared devices? They're motion sensors. We've all seen these. You walk into an office building, there's this little kind of boxy thing in the corner with this little kind of like Perspex lens on it. And when you move around, the little LED light turns on. passive infrared devices are motion sensors that's what we use them for sensing motion how do they sense motion well we are warm-blooded mammals and because we're warm-blooded mammals typically you know for a room like i'm in right now which is not that warm I'm radiating infrared light. The heat from my skin is radiating outward. I'm radiating infrared light. Now we obviously can't see that with our eyes, but... passive infrared devices are basically really low resolution cameras. And they're taking a picture of the room every few milliseconds. And then they're just comparing frame to frame. And if you're moving through the room, then the infrared light signature in the room is going to change from frame to frame. And that's how a passive infrared device can detect motion. It's taking a picture, basically a really low resolution picture of the infrared light in a room. And if we as warm-blooded mammals or other things are moving around, the infrared light signature in the room is going to change. So that's how they detect motion. Which leads to one other important point about these things. They need to be able to recalibrate themselves as the ambient air temperature changes. And the perfect example I always think of this is John and I were teaching a class in Phoenix, Arizona many years ago. i remember it was in the summertime and if you've ever been to phoenix arizona in the summertime i think it's about the same temperature as the surface of the sun it's insanely hot and i remember we were at this client's campus and we were taking a lunch break and we were on this outdoor patio and there was actually a little passive infrared device out on the lunch patio and i remember looking at it thinking Huh. Because normally, how would one of these things sense us moving around in the room? Well, they would actually be looking for an increase in the amount of infrared light in a room, right? Because our bodies are giving off heat. But in Phoenix, Arizona in the summertime, the ambient air temperature is hotter than your skin temperature. And so what that passive infrared device was looking for on that outdoor patio was if I walked by it, it wasn't actually looking for an increase in the amount of infrared light, it was actually looking for a decrease. of me walking by so the point is these things this kind of an extreme example but these things need to be able to recalibrate themselves as the ambient air temperature changes because the um you know the infrared light signature in the room is going to be changing so just remember that they need to recalibrate themselves as the ambient air temperature changes Okay. Lighting. Lighting is actually a really good physical security control for a few reasons. Lighting is really important for the safety of people. If you have like an employee parking lot and people are leaving their offices after dark and going out into the employee parking lot, If you don't have a well-lit parking lot, there's a much higher chance that someone's going to fall and hurt themselves. Also, lighting is a very good deterrent. It's well proven that a well-lit area is much less susceptible to crime. So again, this is good for the safety of people. If you have a well-lit parking lot, there's much less of a chance that one of your employees is going to get mugged or something. Lighting is really good for the safety of people. It's also a really good deterrent, as I mentioned. If you have a well-lit area all around your building, it's way less likely that someone's going to come up and vandalize something or try and steal something. So lighting is a good deterrent. It also, to a degree, helps with detection. Like, yes, there are camera systems that will work with infrared light. They can work at nighttime. But if you've ever looked at sort of an infrared view from your camera system, the image is always a lot more grainy, you don't have any colour, it's all black and white. It's a lot harder to see what's going on. If you have a well-lit area, your cameras can stay in sort of daytime mode, and they can be recording higher resolution, colour images, things like that. And if you have guards that are making their rounds and things, they can much more easily see if someone's doing something they're not supposed to. So lighting even helps from a detection perspective. Makes it so you can get better resolution, better images from your cameras, and your guards and things like that can see better. So that's actually why lighting is a really important physical security control. Good for the safety of people. Good deterrent. Helps with detection. He'll talk about card readers and badges. There's a subtle difference between these that you need to know. Typically conflate these two. Here's why. I'll show you what the definitions are. So let's start with a badge. A badge is just a name and a photo printed on a card. There's no electronics involved here. It's just a name and a photo printed on a card. And the way that a badge is meant to work is if you're trying to go into a secure building, you would show your badge to the guard. The guard would look at the picture on the badge, look at you, and try and decide are you the same person or not, and decide whether they're going to let you in. So a badge is just a name and a picture printed on a card. A card reader is an actual electronic system. It might be an old school swipe card, so it might be like a magnetic swipe card that you have to slide through your lock, or it could be a more modern wireless type card, could even be a smart card where you just tap, you know, just put the card near the reader and it'll let you through. The point is the card reader is some sort of electronic system where there's a unique number on the card that's being read or there's a smart chip on the card that's being challenged and responded to. and that's what's letting you through. So just remember card readers are actual electronic systems. Now the reason that we tend to conflate badges and card readers is because a lot of you probably have a single card that is both a card reader and A badge. Back when I worked at Deloitte and with lots of clients that we had, they would give me a single card. That card would be the electronic card. And of course, what would be printed on that electronic card? My name and my picture. So it was both a card reader and a badge. Okay, let's talk about doors. Doors are obviously extremely important from a physical security perspective. If you wanna control who can get where in your building, you need doors. So here's a few things you have to think about related to doors. First thing is what's the door made out of? All right, if you want a super secure door, you're not just gonna build it out of balsa wood. You're gonna build, you know, this is why like bank doors, vault doors, right? They're not built out of wood. They're built out of like steel or something, right? The example I always think of when I think about this is, have you watched The Shining? So Shining is this great movie with Jack Nicholson in it. And there's this great scene where Jack Nicholson is outside the door. And then he like smashes through this door in a matter of seconds. And he's like, here's Johnny. It's a super creepy scene. So I was thinking about that scene. If you don't want Jack Nicholson smashing his way into your nightmares, into your home, you want a door that's securely made. You want a door securely constructed, right? big steel door or something like that so obviously what the door is made of has a huge impact on the security of the door same with the door frame right there's no point in having a super secure steel door inside of a rotten wooden frame so you have to think about what the door frame is made of Last important thing to think about is where are the hinges located? I'll give you a perfect example of this. Just recently, we have a little ski condo that we go up to every weekend, my wife and our two daughters. And just a couple of weekends ago, we went up to our ski condo and we have a little locker inside of our ski condo where we keep all of our ski clothes and our helmets and our goggles and all this other stuff. And in order to go skiing, we absolutely need to get into this little locked closet, this little owner's closet. Well, when we went up there, we realized we just put new locks on the owner's locker and we realized that we'd left the key back at our house, which is like a four hour drive away. And so we were feeling pretty bummed about this, right? We've just driven four hours to get up there Friday night. It was snowing, so the next morning there was going to be beautiful snow and it was like, oh man, you know, like I didn't want to drive eight hours through the middle of the night to go and get the key. So we're like, what are we gonna do? And I looked at the door and I realized, aha, like this is just a cheap, normal residential door. And the door swung outwards towards where I was standing. And so that meant the hinges were on the outside. and you're probably, if you've got a nefarious nature, you know where I'm going here. If the door swings out there and you can see the hinges, a lot of doors, you can very easily take the door right off the frame just by removing the pins from the hinge. So I did that. Luckily I had a screwdriver. I just used a screwdriver and just banged the pins out took two pins out of the hinges, just took the entire door off the frame, didn't matter that it was locked, and we were able to get into our owner's locker and we were able to ski. So, got to think about this, right? If you want to build a more secure facility, you've got to think about where the hinges are. You can get more secure hinges where the pin is actually like welded in place, you can get even more secure hinges where they are like built into the door frame itself and there are certain types of doors that have locking mechanisms and that even if you pop the pins out the door is still not going to come out of the frame so depending on how secure of a facility you need to make there's many many increasing levels of more secure hinges and more secure ways of doing this but the point is you want to build a very secure facility you've got to think about this stuff what's the door made out of what's the door frame made out of where are the hinges located etc Last little piece I'm gonna cover here related to doors is man traps, which by the way, super sexist term. We shouldn't be calling these man traps, right? Women can use these things. They should be called like people traps, right? So as an industry, we should be calling these people traps, not man traps. Okay, but for the exam, you do need to know the term man trap. So what is a man trap? A man trap is a type of door that's specifically designed to prevent the social engineering attack known as tailgating or piggybacking. So remember, what is tailgating or piggybacking? This is where an unauthorized person follows an authorized person through the door, right? This happens all the time. And the reason this is a social engineering attack, right? It relies on human kindness, right? If you see, if you sense someone is, if you're like, if you're walking, if I'm the employee, And I have the badge, not the badge, the card reader to get through the door. If I sense that someone is behind me, my natural instinct when I'm walking up to a door and unlocking the door is to hold the door open for the person that's behind me and say, you know, please, please, after you. And this is why tailgating and piggybacking works because... Out of human kindness, we often want to hold the door open for someone. Well, you may not want that. You might want to prevent that from happening in a really secure facility. And this is where man traps come in. Man traps are specifically designed to prevent tailgating or piggybacking. There's two major styles of man traps. One is what's shown here, which is a turn style. This is often like a glass cylinder. And the way this works is you walk up to the big glass cylinder, you unlock it in some way, usually using your employee badge or something, and the glass cylinder will sort of rotate sufficiently to allow you into the cylinder. And now you're standing inside a glass cylinder. And it's not very big. So you're going to notice if someone else is in there with you. The glass cylinder rotates, so you're now locked inside the glass cylinder. Then you usually have to go through a second factor of authentication, maybe like a fingerprint scanner or an iris scanner or something like that. And as long as you are authenticated through that second factor, the glass cylinder will rotate the rest of the way to let you into the secure facility. And often these types of turnstiles, they'll have pressure sensors on the floor so they can detect if two people are standing inside because they're measuring the weight. Although you do have to be careful with these. These are often banned in places like Europe where there's actually privacy controls and you're not supposed to monitor your employees weight. So that's their personal information. So you have to be careful about that. But yeah, that's one style of man trap where it's a turnstile type system. Turnstiles don't work though in say like a data standard where you need to be bringing carts of equipment in. You can't get them through that little glass cylinder. And this is where you'll encounter the second type of man trap where there's a set of two doors and a little room in between them. So the way this works is you'll authenticate to the first door. The first door will open allowing you to get into this little tiny room. The trap. And the way this works is you cannot open the second door until the first door closes behind you. So now you're standing in a little room and you're going to notice that someone else is in that room with you. And often security guards will have a camera facing into that room and they'll make sure if someone's going through the man trap that only one person is going through at a time. So first door opens, you get into the man trap. First door has to close behind you. You then usually perform a second factor of authentication to open the next door and then you can proceed on. And the whole idea here, right, is you're getting trapped in this little room. First door has to close before you can open the second door and you're gonna notice that someone else is in there with you. So that's a set of, you know, sort of double doors. And the advantage of these is that little room is big enough to bring a cart of equipment and new servers or whatever. So that's the style of door that you'll typically see in data centers. You'll often see both. You'll see the turnstile man trap for letting employees in and out quickly. And you'll see the double doors to bring equipment in and out of the data center. So there you go. That is doors. Bye.
